title: LCR ETS Flavor Model — What Makes a Question Feel Like a Real ETS Question
description: Distilled from 16 reference samples + quantitative analysis. This is the definitive guide for prompt builder calibration.

model_1_speaker_sentence_design.title: How to write the speaker sentence

rules.why: This is HEARD not read. Real people speak in short bursts. 56% of samples are 4-6 words.
good: Do you want to get lunch?
I have to tell you something.
bad: I was wondering whether you might perhaps be available to join me for a meal this afternoon.

rules.why: 62.5% of samples use contractions. No contraction = sounds like a textbook.
bad: Did I not just see you...
I am afraid I am not...

rules.target: ~30% statements, ~25% yes/no Q, ~25% wh-Q, ~20% negative Q
rules.why: Statements are hardest (require inferring what response is expected). Mix types to vary difficulty.
rules.statements_are_harder: When the speaker makes a statement, the test-taker must infer the appropriate conversational move WITHOUT a question to guide them.

rules.rule: Each sentence must have a clear communicative purpose

purpose_types.seeking_information: Where is...? How do I...? What time...?
purpose_types.inviting: Do you want to...? Would you like to...?
purpose_types.requesting: Are you done with...? Can you...?
purpose_types.declining_or_stating_unavailability: I'm afraid I'm not... I'm not available...
purpose_types.sharing_feelings_or_intentions: I'm thinking of... I have to tell you...

rules.rule: Embed at least one 'trappable' content word
rules.why: 81% of real items have a word that gets reused in a wrong distractor. The speaker sentence must contain at least 1 content word that can be plausibly misused.

examples.trap_potential: missed the bus, bus route, bus driver

model_2_correct_answer_paradigms.title: The 5 correct answer paradigms — how ETS designs right answers

paradigms.name: context_shift
paradigms.description: The answer shifts context to address the UNDERLYING need, not the surface question.
paradigms.mechanism: Speaker asks X, answer addresses Y (the real need behind X).

examples.speaker: What time do I need to pick up Michael?
examples.answer: Don't worry about it. I'll get him.
examples.why: Instead of answering the time, solves the whole problem.

examples.speaker: Are you going to school today?
examples.why: Instead of yes/no, provides the reason that makes the question moot.

paradigms.prompt_instruction: The correct answer should NOT answer the literal question. Instead, it should address the underlying situation in a way that makes the original question unnecessary.

paradigms.name: idiomatic
paradigms.description: The answer uses an English idiom or conversational formula.
paradigms.mechanism: Natural speakers use formulaic expressions that non-native speakers may not recognize.

examples.speaker: I have to tell you something.
Do you want to go to the concert?
Didn't I just see you in the library?
examples.answer: As a matter of fact, I was returning a book.
examples.why: Discourse marker 'as a matter of fact' signals confirmation with elaboration.

paradigms.prompt_instruction: The correct answer should use a natural English idiom, conversational formula, or discourse marker that native speakers would use but learners might not recognize.

paradigms.name: counter_question
paradigms.description: The answer is itself a question that advances the conversation.
paradigms.mechanism: Instead of answering directly, asks a follow-up that moves things forward.

examples.speaker: I'm afraid I'm not available this evening.
examples.why: Proposes alternative rather than just accepting.

examples.speaker: I'm not available tomorrow.
examples.answer: What's a better day for you?

examples.speaker: I'm thinking of dropping Chemistry.
examples.answer: Maybe you should talk to the professor first?

paradigms.prompt_instruction: The correct answer should be a question that naturally follows from the speaker's statement, advancing the conversation rather than just responding.

paradigms.name: marker_led_indirect
paradigms.description: Opens with a discourse marker, then provides an indirect but relevant response.
paradigms.mechanism: Discourse marker signals the speaker is about to reframe or add nuance.

examples.speaker: Are you done with the printer?
examples.answer: Just one more minute, I'm printing the last page.

paradigms.prompt_instruction: The correct answer should open with a discourse marker (Actually, Well, Just, Maybe, As a matter of fact) followed by a relevant but not entirely direct response.

paradigms.name: direct_topical
paradigms.description: Straightforward, factual answer to a factual question.
paradigms.mechanism: The easiest type — speaker asks for information, answer provides it.

examples.speaker: Do you know if the library is open on Saturdays?
examples.answer: Yes, it opens at 10 AM on weekends.

paradigms.prompt_instruction: Use sparingly (≤30% of items). The correct answer directly and factually answers the question.
paradigms.warning: If ALL correct answers are this type, the item set will feel like a vocabulary test, not a pragmatics test.

model_2_correct_answer_paradigms.critical_insight: Only 6% of real ETS correct answers are 'direct_topical'. If AI generates mostly direct answers, the items will NOT feel like ETS. The challenge is in the INDIRECT paradigms.

model_3_distractor_engineering.title: How to engineer wrong answers that feel ETS-real

principles.principle: Every distractor must be a VALID English sentence in SOME context
principles.why: ETS never uses grammar errors or nonsense as distractors. Each wrong answer would be correct in a different conversation.
principles.measured: 100% of 48 distractors are grammatically correct standalone sentences.

principles.principle: Each item needs exactly 3 distractor types, ideally all different
principles.why: 62.5% of items use 3 different distractor types. Diversity prevents test-taking strategies.

distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.frequency: per-item: 56% of items have at least one
distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.mechanism: Takes a content word from the speaker and builds a response around a RELATED concept, not the actual topic.
distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.formula: speaker word → associated concept → plausible sentence about that concept

examples.trap: The book is on the third floor.
The airport has a new snack shop.
The printer is new and very fast.
Chemistry is a required subject for everyone.

distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.quality_test: If a test-taker ONLY heard the keyword (not the full sentence), would this distractor seem right? If yes, it's a good word trap.

distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.mechanism: A perfectly normal English sentence that has ZERO relevance to the conversation.
distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.formula: Write a sentence about a completely different topic that a person might say in daily life.

examples.speaker: I'm not available this evening.
examples.why: About a different person, different time, different situation.

examples.speaker: How did the presentation go?
examples.trap: Despite the rain, I got a quick walk in after lunch.

examples.speaker: Isn't the post office open today?
examples.trap: I think he's come home already.
examples.why: Different person, different place, different question.

distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.quality_test: If you showed ONLY the distractor without the speaker sentence, would it sound like a normal English sentence? It must pass this test.

distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.mechanism: Answers a DIFFERENT type of question than what was asked.
distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.formula: Speaker asks 'where' → distractor answers 'when'. Speaker asks 'how' → distractor answers 'yes/no'.

examples.example: Yes, you're allowed to do that.

distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.quality_test: Is the distractor a perfect answer to a DIFFERENT but plausible question someone might ask in the same setting?

distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.mechanism: Uses the same word as the speaker but with a DIFFERENT meaning.
distractor_types_ranked_by_importance.quality_test: Can the trap word be interpreted in two genuinely different ways? The speaker uses meaning A; the distractor uses meaning B.

distractor_anti_patterns: Distractor contains grammar errors → NEVER. ETS distractors are always grammatically perfect.
Distractor is obviously absurd → NEVER. Every distractor must sound like something a real person could say.
All 3 distractors are off-topic → NEVER. At least 1 must be a word trap or misinterpretation (it should ALMOST seem right).
Distractor repeats the speaker sentence → NEVER. Word traps use ASSOCIATED words, not exact repetition.
Distractor is the correct answer to a different item → Avoid. Each distractor should be unique to its item.

model_4_conversation_naturalness.title: What makes a correct answer feel 'natural' in conversation

factors.description: The correct answer keeps the conversation MOVING. It doesn't dead-end.
factors.measured: 31% of correct answers enable a clear next turn. 38% acknowledge and redirect. Only 6% are bare facts.
factors.good: How about tomorrow then? (invites next turn)

factors.description: Casual speaker → casual answer. Formal speaker → slightly formal answer.
factors.measured: 69% neutral, 19% casual-warm, 12% enthusiastic. ZERO formal or cold.
factors.good: I'm not hungry. (matches casual 'Do you want to get lunch?')
factors.bad: I regret to inform you that I am not presently experiencing hunger.

factors.description: When the speaker expresses frustration/concern, the correct answer acknowledges the emotion before addressing content.

examples.speaker: I'm thinking of dropping Chemistry. It's just too hard.
examples.answer: Maybe you should talk to the professor first?
examples.why: 'Maybe' softens the advice, acknowledging the difficulty.

factors.description: Correct answers give JUST ENOUGH information — never too much, never too little.
factors.measured: Average 5.7 words. Maximum 10 words. 37.5% are ≤4 words.
factors.good: Don't worry about it. I'll get him.
factors.bad: Well actually I was planning to drive to the airport myself anyway since I need to pick up a package from the cargo terminal so I might as well get Michael too.

factors.description: Opening discourse markers signal HOW the answer relates to the question.

marker_meanings.Actually: I'm about to correct an assumption or add unexpected info
marker_meanings.Well: I'm about to give a nuanced or reluctant response
marker_meanings.Don't worry: I'm going to solve this for you

factors.measured: 37.5% of correct answers use these markers

model_5_difficulty_levers.title: How to control difficulty — the 4 levers

levers.easy: Direct factual answer ('Yes, it opens at 10 AM')
levers.medium: Indirect but clearly related ('Actually, today is a holiday')
levers.hard: Very indirect — addresses underlying need, not surface question ('Don't worry, I'll get him')

levers.easy: Word trap uses obviously wrong context
levers.medium: Word trap uses plausible but wrong context
levers.hard: Word trap uses polysemy (same word, different meaning: 'tell' → 'tell the difference')
Declarative statement requiring intent inference ('I have to tell you something')

levers.medium: Common discourse markers (Actually, Well, How about)
levers.hard: Idiom in correct answer (I'm all ears, Absolutely, As a matter of fact)

easy.levers: direct answer + clear question + weak traps

medium.levers: indirect answer + negative Q or statement + moderate traps + discourse markers

hard.levers: very indirect answer + statement speaker + polysemy traps + idioms

model_6_item_design_checklist.title: Checklist for each generated LCR item
pre_flight: Speaker sentence uses natural spoken English (contractions OK)?
Speaker sentence has at least 1 'trappable' content word?
Speaker sentence has a clear communicative purpose?
Correct answer addresses the INTENT, not just literal words?
Correct answer would feel natural if spoken aloud?
At least 1 distractor is a word trap (reuses speaker keyword in wrong context)?
At least 1 distractor is off-topic but grammatically perfect?
3 distractors use at least 2 different distractor types?
All 4 options are similar length (within 5 words of each other)?
Correct answer is NOT consistently the longest option?
Correct answer is NOT a bare 'yes' or 'no'?
The item tests pragmatic understanding, not vocabulary?
